data.title: Yippy Yields And Dollar Dilemmas
data.description:
By Agatha Malinowski

Equity markets have made a remarkable recovery following a turbulent start to the second quarter, with the S&P 500® closing out May up 6%. However, ongoing uncertainties surrounding tariffs and their inflationary impact, concerns about the fiscal deficit and potential shifts in foreign demand for U.S. Treasuries have weighed on the bond market, with the iBoxx $ Treasuries down 1% in May — marking its first monthly loss this year.

Despite declines across the yield curve on June 4, 2025, following a soft labor market report, 2-Year and 30-Year U.S. Treasury yields have surged by over 23 and 27 bps,1 respectively, since the end of April, with the 30-Year Treasury yield briefly surpassing 5% following weak demand in the 20-Year U.S. Treasury auction. As a result, the spread between short- and long-term U.S. Treasury yields has widened, suggesting that investors may be demanding greater compensation for holding longer-term debt. Exhibit 1 highlights the spread between 2-Year and 30-Year U.S. Treasury yields, which has increased to over 100 bps, reaching levels not seen since 2022.

Rising yields also may affect the potential for diversification across asset classes. Traditionally, bonds have tended to exhibit an inverse correlation to equities, with notable outliers such as most of 2024. However, after witnessing negative correlations so far this year, Exhibit 2 reveals that the six-month correlations between The 500™ and the S&P U.S. Treasury Bond Current 30-Year Index have turned positive. If sustained, a positive relationship could be indicative of a potential headwind for risk-adjusted performance for combinations of both asset classes.

In addition to their impact on diversification, we can also examine the currency dynamics associated with an increase in bond yields, which could be particularly relevant for global asset owners who may be affected by currency hedging decisions. Notably, despite the rise in long-term U.S. Treasury yields, the U.S. dollar has weakened, with the S&P U.S. Dollar Futures Index down 4% quarter-to-date. Although the dollar stabilized in May, the currency’s weakness coinciding with rising bonds yields marks a departure from their typical positive relationship, as shown in Exhibit 3. Recent risks, including fiscal concerns and doubts about the dollar’s safe haven status, may have contributed to this diverging trend.

While the future trajectory of U.S. Treasury yields remains uncertain, we can look to market expectations for long-dated U.S. Treasury volatility, as measured by the options market with the Cboe 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF Volatility Index (VXTLT). After declining from the highs observed during the market turbulence in April, Exhibit 4 shows that VXTLT rose along with longer-dated yields, surpassing the 20 mark on May 21, 2025, and closed above the 17 handle as of June 4.

Looking ahead, although numerous unknowns remain, the impact of U.S. Treasury yields on the term premium, along with the associated diversification and currency dynamics, could have significant implications for asset owners across asset classes.

1 Data as of June 4, 2025.

data.title: Centene: Strong Execution And Margin Discipline Support Potential Upside
data.description:
Centene (CNC) is one of those companies in the medical services market that is overlooked in my opinion. This medical giant manages the largest portfolio of Medicaid policies in the country, dominates the ACA insurance exchanges, and leads the Medicare Part D drug coverage market, serving nearly 29 million insured people as of 2024 end. That is why it is definitely worth taking a closer look at the essence of Centene's business, delving into its finances, and trying to assess the fair value of the shares.

From Medicaid Leader To Diversified Insurer

Centene's business model is quite simple: the company sells medical policies under contracts with the US government and just to private clients. After signing a contract with the state, the company receives a fixed premium from the states in which it operates or the federal government, and in exchange undertakes to cover the medical expenses of program participants. In order for the profit to remain in this scheme, Centene must keep payments to participants below the bonuses they receive. Accordingly, the more insured people there are, the more profitable the business is. There are now more than 29 million such insured people. It turns out that the key for the company is the ability to effectively control costs and act systematically: managing drug programs, prevention, and reducing repeat hospitalizations. Additionally, Centene can receive bonuses from Medicare for high-quality ratings.

More than half of the revenue in 2024 came from the Medicaid program ($83.8 billion, 57.6%), followed by the Commercial segment ($33.7 billion, 23.2%), Medicare ($23 billion, 15.8%) and the rest ($4.9 billion, 3.4%). The growth rate of the largest segment is slowing down due to the fact that after the pandemic, the US government decided to conduct an audit of insured persons and some of the program participants were denied access to Medicaid. Centene's management expects tariff increases in 2025-2026 to offset costs. The Medicare segment is actively growing, especially in PDP, where Centene is the de facto market leader. In the first quarter of 2025, PDP membership grew by 22%. Marketplace is also not slowing down: almost 2 million new members were added in just one quarter.

What Centene's management has succeeded in, and for which the management can be praised, is the transition from a Medicaid-focused business model to a diversified multi-format insurer. The purchase of WellCare in 2020 also helped, which boosted Medicare revenues. And getting rid of non-core assets, such as Circle Health, allowed us to focus.

The market in which Centene operates is huge, with an estimated value of $3.5 trillion in 2024, but it is also very competitive. And she has strong competitors. In Medicaid — Elevation, UnitedHealth, Molina, CVS/Aetna. In Medicare Advantage — United Healthcare (UNH), Humana (HUM), and CVS Health (CVS). In Part D, Centene is the leader. Centene is among the largest in the Marketplace, especially with the Ambetter brand. But each of the competitors is strong in its segment, and the struggle is for shares, for customer retention, and for margins.

Strong Results For 1Q 2025

Centene reported well for the first quarter, showing significant revenue growth. Total revenue was $48.6 billion, with consensus expectations of $42.78 billion. The number of insured people on the Marketplace increased by 29% or 1.9 million new members. Similarly, in Medicare Part D, the increase in participants was +22%. In the Medicaid segment, the number of participants remained approximately stable (about 12.9–13.0 million), while the company managed to get about a +4% increase in rates, taking into account individual tariff updates. The commercial segment also showed significant growth due to Marketplace (ACA plans) and a small increase in corporate customers.

Centene's operating profit increased due to business scaling and a reduction in the share of management costs. EBIT was about $1.53 billion compared to $1.1 billion a year earlier (+39%). The EBIT margin was about 3.6% (compared to 3.0% in Q1 2024). The main reason for the improvement was reduced SG&A expenses relative to revenue: the SG&A ratio fell to 7.9% in the quarter (compared to 8.7% in Q1 2024), due to savings on administrative costs and economies of scale. D&A accounted for about 0.75% of revenue, so the EBITDA margin also remained at about 4-4.5%. In total, due to faster revenue growth compared to costs, the operating margin increased. Centene's net profit was $1.3 billion, compared to $1.163 million a year earlier. Diluted EPS increased from $2.16 to $2.63 (+22%). The net income margin remained unchanged at about 2.8–3.0% in the quarter, reflecting proportional growth in profit and revenue. Operational improvements were the main factor in net profit growth; interest expenses decreased slightly – by $8 million to $170 million – and the tax burden increased (the effective rate in the quarter was 24.7%).

Based on the strong start to the year and the current dynamics, Centene management has adjusted its forecasts for 2025. The main change is an increase in the revenue forecast for Premium & Service to the range of $164-166 billion (previously estimated at $158-160 billion). A $6 billion increase from the initial forecast was mainly due to additional revenue of $5 billion from Marketplace and stronger-than-expected results in Medicare Advantage ($1 billion). At the same time, the management did not change the forecast for adjusted EPS – it is still expected to be above $7.25. The CFO of Centene outlined three key factors underlying the forecast for 2025. First, a total increase of 4% is expected in the Medicaid segment, which will help offset the increase in medical costs. Secondly, in Medicare Advantage, the company adheres to the "inverted slope" strategy — low HBR at the beginning of the year, growth by the end, but maintaining annual margins. Third, despite the increased costs associated with the expansion of the network, Centene expects to improve operational efficiency and achieve its stated financial goals.

In my opinion, management's forecasts for 2025 look reasonable in the baseline scenario, but their implementation directly depends on the external environment, which the company only partially controls. The expected 4% increase in Medicaid rates may not cover the ongoing pressure on HBR, especially if states are slow to review and update medical rates — that is, they are delayed in responding to rising costs. The forecast for continued profitability in the Medicare Advantage segment also raises questions: a strategy with low medical costs at the beginning of the year and their growth by the end may lead to stronger margin fluctuations than management currently anticipates, especially against the background of changes in program rules after the IRA reform. Additionally, the retention or growth of the number of insured persons in the context of redetermination in Medicaid and increased competition in the Marketplace is a factor with a high level of uncertainty.

FCF Analysis And Valuation

Centene shares have been falling in value over the past year for several reasons: a decrease in the number of insured in Medicaid, rising medical costs [HBR], increased competition in Medicare Advantage, and market concerns about regulatory and tariff risks amid political uncertainty. The stock is already trading more than 20% cheaper than it was a year ago, and I think this is a good chance to take advantage of the moment and buy the oversold shares.

I built a DCF model with a forecast of UFCF up to 2030 in order to identify the fair value of Centene shares. The results of the UFCF calculations can be seen in the table below.

In my model, the cash flow forecast for Centene is based on the following key assumptions:

  • Revenue: I expect Centene's revenue to grow significantly in 2025, from $147.2 billion in 2024 to $168.1 billion in 2025 (an increase of +14.2%). Such a sharp increase is due to the fact that in Q1, the company significantly exceeded initial expectations for membership in the Obamacare and Medicare market (which was reflected in the increase in revenue forecast). After 2025, the growth rate will slow down to 5-8%. This scenario corresponds to a combination of the following factors: a sharp initial increase in the membership base (after the success of the marketplace and drug plans) followed by market consolidation. The revenue CAGR for the period 2025-2030 will be about 6.6%. In fact, this dynamic suggests that most of the additional participants have already been "captured" by 2025, and further growth is based on more moderate rates and insignificant organic gains.
  • EBIT: I predict that operating profit will grow approximately in proportion to revenue, while maintaining an operating margin of about 3.3–3.4%. Despite the increase in medical costs, I assume that due to savings on SG&A (which can already be seen from the latest results: SG&A's share of revenue dropped to 7.9% in Q1) and a decrease in HBR on average across groups (plus economies of scale), the operating margin will remain. Thus, the model assumes that Centene will maintain a high degree of operational efficiency in the coming years: additional revenues go almost directly to profit, while the total operating margin remains stable.
  • CapEx: In the model, I have included a very low level of capital investment for Centene – about 0.8–1.0% of revenue per year. This reflects the specifics of the industry: insurance companies do not need large investments in fixed assets. Centene spends most of its expenses on IT, mergers and acquisitions, and compliance with regulations – this scales with the business, but remains a small share of revenue. The forecast is focused on maintaining the current IT infrastructure and adding new systems (for example, PBM integration after the transition to CVS, improved analytics) - all this requires capital, but within about 1% of revenue annually.

Thus, according to my forecast, FCF will stay in the range of 1-2% of total revenue, which is actually very conservative, since the average FCF yield for the period in 2020-2024 was about 3.88%, and in some years it even reached 7%. But at the same time, I did not try to specifically underestimate FCF for excessive conservatism, but simply objectively assessed the future and the risks that Centene's business might face.

Further, when calculating the terminal cost, I use three approaches: the P/E multiplier method, the EV/EBITDA method and the perpetuity growth method. This allows you to estimate the range of possible fair value figures, depending on the selected parameters and the level of the discount rate. I got the WACC at 5%, which is quite low and is primarily due to the company's low five-year beta, which was about 0.46x. Plus, the Cost of debt was only 3.8%. I use the latest TTM Interest Expense divided by the latest one-year quarterly average debt to get the simplified cost of debt. The company's debt burden is small, and interest expenses are correspondingly low, so these results come out. In any case, I used several WACC values to simulate the stock price under different scenarios.

The consensus expects a P/E of 7.61x for 2025, so I use three scenarios in my calculations: a low P/E of 6x, a baseline of 7x, and an optimistic P/E of 8x. In the baseline scenario, with a WACC of 5.0% and a P/E of about 7x, the fair share price will be about $63 with an upside of 13%. In the worst-case scenario, when the WACC is 1% higher and the P/E is even lower (6x), a downside of up to 5% is formed. Thus, with the most positive inputs, the share price will be 31% higher than the current one ($55.4), and with the most negative ones, it will be 5% lower.

The situation is similar with the EV/EBITDA multiplier. Here, in the base scenario (WACC – 5%, EV/EBITDA – 6x), the fair price will be about $59 with a fairly limited upside of 7%. At the same time, with the most conservative input data, the price may fall by 13%, and with the most positive, it may rise by 27%.

The share price sensitivity table calculated using the Perpetuity Growth Method shows us an even more limited price upside of 6% in the baseline scenario, and in the worst-case scenario, if the WACC increases to 6% and the growth rate in the terminal period is about 2%, then the fair value of the share will be 7% lower than the current one. A positive scenario gives an upside of 34%.

I took the baseline scenario for each valuation method and calculated the average price among all three methods and got a fair value of the company's shares at $60 with an upside of about 9%. In a negative scenario, the downside is limited to 9%, and in an optimistic scenario, the price may rise by 31% to $72. At the same time, I consider my estimate to be quite conservative, especially in terms of calculating FCF. I assign a "Buy" rating to Centene.

Capital Return To Shareholders

Centene traditionally prefers to return capital to shareholders through share buybacks rather than through dividends. In recent years, it has been necessary to significantly stimulate the return of funds to investors. The board authorized a $4 billion increase to the existing share repurchase program. This was announced alongside raising Centene's 2024 outlook and reaffirming its 2023 guidance. In total, Centene bought about $7.6 billion of its shares in 2022-2024. The year 2024 was particularly active: the report for the 4th quarter of 2024 indicated that the company had bought 42.0 million shares worth $3.0 billion. This made it possible to reduce the number of shares in circulation and increase EPS figures. As of February 2025, there was about $2.2 billion of unused buyback limit, which the company can use if necessary. The terms of the program allow for flexible purchase of shares on the market, and Centene declares the right to suspend buybacks at any time. Thus, the current policy of returning funds to shareholders is through aggressive share buybacks.

Risks

In fact, working with the government creates both advantages and risks, so I would mark government regulation as the first risk. The slightest changes to the rules of Medicaid or Medicare can seriously affect revenue. For example, if states decide to cut funding or increase program requirements, Centene could quickly lose thousands of customers. The introduction of import tariffs by Trump creates pressure on the prices of medical equipment and, consequently, on business margins. You can also add a government review of citizens' rights to health programs, in particular to Medicaid, which led to a reduction in the number of insured persons. The result is already visible in the first quarter of 2025, when the total number of participants decreased by 1.7% year-on-year. At the same time, expenses are growing: the payout ratio (HBR) for Medicaid and commercial segments exceeded 89-93%, which practically reduces profits to zero. If insurance premiums don't rise faster than costs, Centene will be in a difficult position. All this is happening against the backdrop of increasing competition — giants like UnitedHealth and Humana are active in the same segments and are ready to win back market share. Additionally, operational and economic risks create pressure. Calculation failures, changes in CMS requirements, inflation, and high-interest rates can all affect business margins. Centene tries to hedge these threats, but the nature of its business itself implies a high sensitivity to the external environment.

Final Thoughts

In the current environment, Centene remains a systemically important player in the U.S. government health program market with strong positions in Medicaid, Medicare, and ACA marketplaces. The financial results for the first quarter of 2025 turned out to be strong, which allows us to look positively at the revenue growth potential in the current year. However, the implementation of the forecast depends on a number of factors beyond the company's control, from tariff flexibility at the state level to policy decisions in healthcare and inflationary pressures. My assessment shows that under basic assumptions, the fair value of the stock is $60 with an upside of about 9%, and the risk/return profile looks balanced. Given the current oversold securities, margin stabilization and the availability of a buyback program, Centene deserves a Buy rating in my opinion.

data.title: Venture Capital Seeks AI Winners As Private Equity Makes Infrastructure Play
data.description:
Risk is filtering the flow of private capital into AI as venture capital and private equity investors pick different targets in the booming industry.

While venture capital competes to invest in AI, private equity is prioritizing investment in the infrastructure that underpins AI's expansion. Private equity-backed datacenter M&A hit $18.15 billion globally in 2024, the highest total in at least five years, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

There is money to be made on the cutting edge of AI, but investors there risk being left behind by the next disruptive AI innovation, Blue Owl Capital Inc. co-CEO Marc Lipschultz said when the alternative asset manager reported 2024 fourth-quarter earnings in February. "We don't want any of those risks for our investors," Lipschultz said.

"If you believe that 10 years from now, AI will be an important part of the fabric of IT or the way we operate in this economy, then you want the picks and shovels, you want the infrastructure that goes with it," Lipschultz said.

Risk and reward

Private equity and venture capital firms invested $63.97 billion in AI via M&A between 2020 and 2024, according to Market Intelligence data. Over three times as much — a total of $216.51 billion between 2020 and 2024 — was invested in AI companies through rounds of funding, a proxy for venture capital activity.

The different approaches to investing in the fast-growing AI industry reflect the differences in strategy that separate venture capital and private equity. A preference for a lower-risk investment with stable returns is typical of the private equity approach. Venture capital investors aim for higher-risk and likely higher-reward investments in young companies that have the potential to gain a foothold in the market.

"Many of those [early-stage AI] companies, their business model is more amorphous, or more speculative. That is the playground for [venture capital]," said Glenn Mincey, US head of private equity for KPMG.

A $40 billion round of funding raised in March by OpenAI LLC was the largest private equity or venture capital investment targeting AI companies since Jan. 1, 2024.

Datacenters are a safe haven

Cash flow is another key consideration in private equity's picks-and-shovels strategy, said Brad Haller, a senior partner in technology consulting firm West Monroe's M&A practice. Private equity's leveraged buyout playbook is simply designed for more mature businesses.

"A lot of these smaller [AI] companies do not have the positive cash flow to be able to be invested in by a private equity firm that's going to put millions or billions of dollars of debt on their balance sheet that they'll have to service," Haller said.

The outlook for sustained datacenter demand backs private equity's investment thesis. Private equity views datacenters as "long-term safe havens for investments, even during turbulent times," said John Dinsdale, chief analyst and managing director for Synergy Research Group, in emailed comments.

"There has been an inexorable rise in the demand for datacenter capacity, driven by cloud services, social networking and a range of both consumer and enterprise digital services. The rise of generative AI has added a further major boost to demand," Dinsdale said.

Private equity-backed deals have accounted for 80% to 90% of datacenter transaction value since 2022, according to Synergy.

The $16.13 billion acquisition of datacenter operator AirTrunk Operating Pty. Ltd. by Blackstone Inc. and Canada Pension Plan Investment Board was the largest private equity-backed datacenter transaction since Jan. 1, 2024.

Seven of the 10 largest private equity and venture capital investments in datacenters over the measured period came via rounds of funding, not M&A. While it would appear to buck the pattern of venture capital preferring riskier bets on AI startups, West Monroe's Haller suggested it could represent a bit of hedging by venture capital investors.

"A datacenter is a 'Steady Eddie' investment. You’re going to be able to model out a pretty predictable [internal rate of return] for that, and then you can make some more risk-forward bets in other places," Haller said.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Rosenbauer International: A Firefighting Vehicle Giant That's Recovering, But Priced Right
data.description:
Introduction

Rosenbauer International AG (OTC:RSNBF) is among the global leaders delivering firefighting trucks and emergency response solutions — a niche but critical part of the industrial sector. Having experienced supply chain issues and compressed margins, the company has achieved an impressive operational turnaround. Revenues are recovering, the margins are improving, and the cash flows are on the brink of turning positive.

All this said, while there are high hopes for 2025, the price already seems to reflect most of the positives. Factor in low liquidity, recent shareholder dilution, and a new majority owner, and there isn't anything that signals a substantial fair value gap. Based on that, I am assigning a HOLD rating.

Market Position and Operations: World Market Leader in an Established but Niche Market

Together with REV Group (REVG) and Oshkosh Corporation (OSK), Rosenbauer makes up two-thirds of the global fire and emergency vehicle market. The company sells to structurally stable but niche markets that are driven by fleet replacement cycles, infrastructure renewals, and stable public sector demand. The business is not cyclical, but is characteristically order-activity lumpy due to the timing of projects and procurement cycles of regulators.

Its products include firefighting trucks, safety equipment, and preventative protection systems. However, fire protection vehicles remain the key business, even with an increasing focus on higher-margin segments.

Rosenbauer has a geographically diversified footprint. Half of its business comes from Europe, while the Americas represents around 27%, and the remaining portion is between the Middle East and the Asia-Pacific. This diversification protects it from regional funding cycles and provides exposure to long-term procurement contracts in mature and developing economies.

As mentioned, the fire protection business is driven by many factors, like fleet replacement cycles, safety standard upgrades, and infrastructure projects. That's why the recovery in 2024 has been more than just a relaxation of supply pressures. Management believes that growth lies ahead, especially with an increased acceleration of public spending. As they state:

Now that international supply chains have returned to their usual stability, the global firefighting industry should continue growing in 2025. Despite a weak economic environment, the public sector is continuously investing in the safety of people and infrastructure.

That said, Rosenbauer is still tied to its capital-intensive production base, and expansion of the higher-margin segments will become crucially important to its long-term margin expansion.

2024 & Q1 2025: Recovery Underway, but Execution is Critical

Rosenbauer experienced a robust turnaround in 2024, breaking the previous three-year cycle of elevated costs and supply pressures. Full-year revenue grew 22.7% to €1.3 billion, impacted by improved throughput and relieving bottlenecks in production. Operating profitability turned positive, with EBIT increasing to €64.9 million and the margin improving to 5.0% versus 3.5% last year.

Metric 2024 2023 YoY Change
Revenue €1.305 billion €1.064 billion 22.7%
EBITDA €97.2 million €67.7 million 43.6%
EBIT €64.9 million €37.5 million 73.1%
Pre-Tax Income €26.3 million €7 million 275.7%
Net Income €29.8 million €1.2 million 2383.3%

Free cash flow also improved considerably. The company decreased its working capital requirements and began to work down inventory, resulting in a free cash flow of €47 million. The net income amounted to €29.8 million, compared to €1.2 million in 2023, without an adjustment for the impact of an exceptional item on tax.

Metric Q1 2025 Q1 2024 YoY Change
Revenue €263.6 million €225.6 million 16.9%
EBITDA €16.7 million €7.6 million 119.7%
EBIT €9.0 million €0.3 million 2900%
Pre-Tax Income €1.8 million €-9.0 million Turned positive.
Net Income €0.1 million €-9.1 million Turned positive.

The momentum carried forward into a seasonably weaker Q1 2025. Revenue increased 16.8 percent year-over-year to €263.6 million, and EBIT returned to €9.0 million — a stark reversal from last year's breakeven. Operating cash flow remained modestly negative at –€6.8 million, but the shortfall was significantly reduced compared with –€58.4 million last year, as better production flow offset the timing of payments. This proves that execution is holding even as volumes ramp up.

The order book stood at an all-time high of €2.34 billion at the end of Q1 2025, which equals 18 to 20 months of business at prevailing run rates. Having secured its long-term projects in Germany, Romania, and the Middle East region, the group has good revenue visibility to take forward into the latter part of the year.

2025 Guidance: Top-Line Growth and Margin Expansion

In the face of all this, the company is forecasting 2025 revenue of €1.5 billion and an EBIT margin of 6%. That would translate to around €90 million of operating income — 39% growth from 2024. At the bottom line, even the historical 2.3% net margin would translate to around €34.5 million, or a 15.8% increase. A more realistic scenario is with a 3.3%, then they should be closer to €50 million — that's a 68% increase from the €29.8 million last year.

However, operating leverage remains the key wild card. With the production situation normalizing and financing costs trending down following the refinancing, the stage is set for incremental margin growth if the execution remains on track. Even the base case looks nicely supported by the existing backlog and operating momentum.

Financial Situation: Stabilized but Far From Smooth

The move that set the stage for 2025 happened in early February. Rosenbauer completed an equity raise of €119 million by issuing 3.4 million shares (€35 per share) and diluting the existing shareholders by around 33%. Although this might be seen as a negative by some of the investors, it improved the equity base and introduced a new exclusive shareholder.

The injection of capital lowered net debt from €492.8 million in Q1 2024 to €288 million in Q1 2025. That's a significant improvement in such a short time, even if it's at the cost of dilution. As of March 2025, the equity ratio now stands at 25.2%, up from 13.5% in 2024. The company is now structurally stronger, with just enough space to breathe.

In addition, the equity raise allowed the company to replace its existing debt with a new, more flexible €330 million syndicated loan facility and an extension option until 2028. That is a significant plus because it decreases interest costs, provides more liquidity, and reduces covenant tension, like a previous ban on dividend payments.

All of this said, the balance sheet is not yet out of the woods, as Rosenbauer remains a capital-intensive company with a high level of leverage. As of Q1 2025, operating cash flow remains negative, primarily because of the ramp-up of production and higher inventories. However, the company is expecting positive operating cash flow for this year. If they meet guidance, the worst of the working capital pressure may be behind it.

Valuation: Reasonably Priced on Trailing Numbers, but No Apparent Discount on Forward Prospects

At a current price of €40.80 and with the new capital increase, Rosenbauer’s market cap is now around €416 million. With a TTM net income of €39 million, this puts the company at a P/E multiple of 10.7x. On an EV/EBITDA basis, the company is at 6.6x, stripping out the now-reduced net debt.

Company P/S P/E EV/EBITDA Revenue Growth (YoY) Net Income Growth (YoY)
Rosenbauer International AG 0.31 10.7 6.6 22.7% 2383%
Oshkosh Corporation 0.60 10.3 6.7 11.4% 13.9%
REV Group, Inc. 0.83 20.6 11.8 -9.8% 468.6%
Morita Holdings Corporation (6455.T) 0.87 10.3 4.2 17.4% 57.6%

When compared to its competitors, Rosenbauer doesn't look mispriced. For example, Oshkosh Corporation, a specialty truck and defense vehicle producer is trading at 10.3x P/E and 6.7x EV/EBITDA, virtually the same ratio as Rosenbauer. Japan's Morita Holdings, a more local firefighting peer, has a 10.3x P/E but lower EV/EBITDA at 4.2x. REV Group, despite negative revenue growth, has higher multiples — 20.6x P/E and 11.8x EV/EBITDA.

In terms of revenue growth, Rosenbauer is also performing relatively well. In 2024, it reported 22.7% top-line growth, which is higher than both Oshkosh's 11.4% and Morita's 17.4%. REV Group, on the other hand, actually experienced a decrease of around 10%, so part of its higher multiple is likely hope for the company's restructuring or growth.

Based on the forward multiples, the situation remains the same. If we assume that Rosenbauer will meet its 2025 guidance, then that puts the net income in the range of €45–50 million or 8.3x–9.2x forward P/E. On paper, that is attractive, assuming interest expense falls further, and operating leverage comes through in the latter half of the year. However, that is not the case if we look at the guidance of peers. Oshkosh expects solid growth, REV is banking on a turnaround, and Morita is expected to remain flat. Factoring in these forecasts and risks, the forward P/E does not signal undervaluation. I believe it is fairly valued — not expensive, but not a bargain either.

Simply put, Rosenbauer's valuation reflects its operating turnaround with its current risks. The execution is improving and growth is sensible, but the upside is already reflected. Without the more substantial discount relative to peers, I don't see a proper entry point yet. At this price, the shares look roughly equally weighted between risk and opportunity.

Catalysts: Execution and Visibility Could Support a Reassessment

Rosenbauer entered 2025 with positive momentum. The order book stands at a record high of €2.34 billion — more than 18 months of production at current run rates. With production schedules returning to normal and supply chains stabilizing, Rosenbauer should now have the ability to convert this order book to support both revenue and margin expansion.

Internally, the further roll-out of SAP S/4HANA is another key lever. For some background, this is the next-generation ERP system used to run everything from the supply chain to the books. It’s a complicated and costly replacement, but when completed, it should drive the automation, push waste out, and allow tighter working capital control. With such a capital-hungry business, even marginal efficiency gains can have a big impact on the bottom line.

Electrification is also a catalyst. Prep-series production of the battery-electric PANTHER 6x6 has already been ordered by U.S. and New Zealand airports. Demand, while remaining niche, is increasing for low-emission rescue units — especially from wealthier markets with green procurement policies. Rosenbauer's head start may pay dividends if the trend picks up speed.

Also on the agenda is governance. The new largest shareholder Robau Beteiligungsverwaltung GmbH may bring some changes — reinstating dividends, improving communication with investors or uplisting. However, this is currently only at a speculation level.

And, ultimately, with raw material costs leveling off and logistics costs below their 2022 peaks, overall gross profit margins should rise — provided there are no trade tensions and no tariffs, specifically EU-U.S. industrial policy tensions or component procurement constraints.

Risks: Structural and Execution Headwinds May Cap Near-Term Upside

Even with cleaner balance sheets and operating efficiencies, the investment case for Rosenbauer is not frictionless. The biggest risk still remains the risk of execution. Scaling to meet a €2.3 billion backlog, rolling out SAP, and absorbing cost pressures while managing the capital-intensive model — might be too much to accomplish. Any slip-up or delay might eat into the margins.

Finance expenses are another drag factor. Despite the refinancing and capital raising, interest still ate heavily into the bottom line during 2024. As long as the generation of the cash improves in the near term, leverage remains an anchor to flexibility — primarily to reinvestment and return on capital.

And there's procurement risk. Rosenbauer is exposed to a high number of deals with national governments, particularly in Germany and Romania. These agreements are typically politically sensitive, and delays or reshuffles tied to election cycles or budget pressures can throw the delivery off schedule. That's the risk whenever you're exposed to state capex cycles.

The current trade and tariff situation also brings some uncertainty. Although input costs have stabilized, and much Rosenbauer sourcing is regionally matched, the business is still exposed to global flows. Since industrial policy tensions between the US and EU are yet to be resolved, further tariffs or bans on critical components such as chassis or batteries have the potential to affect the supply base or erode margins.

Macro risk should not be underestimated as well. The fire and emergency vehicle market is not highly sensitive to the overall economy, but it is still subject to public budget decisions. The slowdown in European infrastructural spending or municipal austerity programs may reduce new order pipelines — exactly when Rosenbauer is gearing up to grow. Pair this with uncertainty around inflation, climate disasters, or global commodity price shocks, and visibility into the future vanishes.

Lastly, the structural setup still holds the stock back. Free float remains at 23.5%, and the average daily volume is well under 2,000 shares, limiting the ability to trade and forcing institutions to stay on the sidelines. Governance remains tight following the entire amount of the 2025 equity offering that was subscribed by a single buyer, Robau Beteiligungsverwaltung GmbH, who now has the controlling ownership of the company. The refinancing agreement also includes cash sweep provisions and equity-linked triggers, which will further dilute equity in the event of disappointing cash flow. None of this is the death knell, but they hold any near-term upside in check.

Conclusion

Rosenbauer has visibly improved over the past twelve months. The recovery in 2024 was more than optical - growth in revenue and margins benefited from improved throughput, cost discipline, and an improving backlog. Cash flow turned a corner, the equity issuance stabilized the balance sheet, and operational execution seemed more robust than in earlier years. The company is also implementing new internal systems and laying the foundation for more substantial longer-term efficiency gains. Taken all together, the company seems healthier and more consistent than in previous years.

With that said, a lot of this recovery is already factored into the stock. Forward multiples aren't expensive, yet they don't necessarily imply a great discount — at least, not with thin liquidity, ownership concentration, and sensitivity to state-controlled procurement trends. However, I think that Rosenbauer can become attractive at some point in the future, but for now, I’m assigning a Hold rating.

data.title: Hong Kong To Maintain Openness And Global Connectivity Amid Trade Conflicts
data.description:
Hong Kong wants to grasp the opportunities resulting from ongoing trade tensions, the city’s Financial Secretary Paul Chan said at a S&P Global Market Intelligence roadshow.

This as Hong Kong maintains its openness and global connectivity through "bold" reforms to deepen and diversify its financial markets, Chan said.

"We believe this is where international investors want to be," Chan said at the June 5 roadshow in Hong Kong. "This optimism is underpinned by our robust fundamentals: large fiscal reserves, sound external balance sheet, resilient monetary system and a track record of transparent and effective policymaking," Chan said.

As a free port reliant on global trade flows, Hong Kong is caught in the trade tensions playing out between mainland China and the US. Starting in early April, China and the US announced a series of tariff moves on each other, which eventually pushed the world's two biggest economies to declare that they would tax imports by up to 145%. On May 12, the US and China agreed to de-escalate the situation by delaying the proposed tariffs by 90 days while negotiators worked on a trade deal.

Chan said the roadshow came as global economic and geopolitical landscapes "undergoing profound transformation, driven by both short-term shocks and long-term structural shifts."

"In the near term, it is the tariff war and the drastic, flip-flopping policies of the Trump Administration which have contributed to great uncertainty and heightened market volatility," Chan said. "Beyond these immediate disruptions, we are witnessing deeper, more enduring trends that will shape the global order for the decade to come."

But investor sentiment has proven resilient and strengthened in Hong Kong, according to Chan, with the stock market rising, bank deposits growing and Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing Ltd. being the top IPO market globally year-to-date.

"The recent inflow of capital into Hong Kong has demonstrated that our fundamentals are robust and trusted by investors. We serve as a safe harbor for international capital," Chan said.

Hong Kong's stock market rose around 18% in 2024 and has gained more than 15% so far this year, Chan said. IPO fundraising has reached HK$77 billion, making Hong Kong the top IPO market globally year-to-date. Bank deposits grew by 7% in 2024 and an additional 4% this year. They now exceed HK$18 trillion, he said.

Original Post
